column1: 3^"Applications Registered to Tenant with Client Secret (Password) Credentials"^"app - CredentialName: sec ValidFrom: 03/11/2026 09:04:04 ValidTo: 09/07/2026 09:04:04"^"Registered applications within the Tenant can grant excessive OAuth permissions to the associated application and in turn grants those permissions to whomever is controlling the application. Registered Applications that leverage client secrets (application password credentials) generally bypass MFA and Conditional Access Policies
column2: and may allow attackers to authenticate as the application and perform any actions the application is capable of executing without triggering alerts. With sufficient access and privileges attackers may obtain
column3: brute force
column4: or add credentials to existing applications in the environment."^"Not Applicable"^"None"^"Critical"^" "^"Remove unapproved applications and review OAuth permission grants. Soteria recommends disabling authentication via client secrets and leveraging certificate authentication instead. This method allows for greater control over the ability to authenticate and impersonate applications within the tenant. Organizations should audit application and service principal credentials regularly to ensure all credentials are valid and expected. Tenants licensed for Advanced Hunting (see Licensing Requirements in the reference links below) may use the KQL query linked in the references below to query for events and create a custom detection rule based on the query."^"ABUSING AZURE APPLICATION CREDENTIALS TO ATTACK SUPPLY CHAINS : https://www.secureworks.com/research/abusing-azure-application-credentials-to-attack-supply-chains;How to Backdoor Azure Applications and Abuse Service Principals : https://www.inversecos.com/2021/10/how-to-backdoor-azure-applications-and.html;Creating a Malicious Azure AD OAuth2 Application : https://trustedsec.com/blog/creating-a-malicious-azure-ad-oauth2-application;Microsoft Defender XDR prerequisites : https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-365/security/defender/prerequisites?view=o365-worldwide#licensing-requirements;Add Credential to App or Service Principal.kql : https://github.com/ThoughtContagion/Useful_KQL_Queries/blob/main/Add%20Credential%20to%20App%20or%20Service%20Principal.kql"^" "^" "^" "^" "^" "

column1: 6^"Dangerous Attachment Extensions are Not Filtered"^"File Types not filtered: wll
column2: rtf
column3: gadget
column29: sldm; Filter name: Default"^"Email is a primary vector of exploitation. It is common for attackers to send malicious file attachments designed to mimic legitimate business files. A list of historically malicious extensions that should be blocked/filtered from O365 emails is checked against the Tenant's malware filters to determine if these file types are being blocked. The file extensions listed herein are on this list of dangerous file extensions
column30: but no O365 Malware Filter Policy is configured to filter them. Creating filters for these file types may decrease the risk of malware spreading within the organization through phishing or lateral phishing. The common malicious attachments defined in O365 at the time this document was authored are: xll
column75: sldm."^"None"^"None"^"Critical"^" "^"This feature is accessible in the Security portal of the O365 Admin Center. Click through to Threat management → Policy → Anti-malware
column76: and either edit the Default policy to include the above extensions
column77: or create a custom policy to filter these extensions. Additionally
column78: if other known dangerous attachment types are added to this exceptions list
column79: they may be quickly filtered. However it is recommended to create a new policy to accomplish this as it is a more ideal long-term solution. Before doing this
column80: consider polling key stakeholders in the organization or using available data to determine whether any of these file types are commonly exchanged via email within the organization. The complete list of additional file types may be added using the listed PowerShell commands."^"Anti-malware Policies : https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-365/security/office-365-security/anti-malware-protection?view=o365-worldwide#anti-malware-policies;50+ File Extensions That Are Potentially Dangerous on Windows : https://www.howtogeek.com/137270/50-file-extensions-that-are-potentially-dangerous-on-windows/;Set-MalwareFilterPolicy : https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/exchange/set-malwarefilterpolicy?view=exchange-ps"^" "^" "^" "^" "^" "

column1: 7^"Dangerous Default Permissions"^"No Conditional Access Policy exists to restrict non-administrator access to Azure Active Directory or Entra. Users can read all attributes in Azure AD Users can create security groups Users are allowed to create and register applications Users with a verified mail domain can join the tenant Guests can invite other guests into the tenant Users are allowed to create new Azure Active Directory Tenants."^"Dangerous default configuration settings were found in the Tenant. By default
column2: Azure tenants allow all users to access the Azure Active Directory blade
column3: to read all other users' accounts
column5: and invite guests. These default settings extend to guest accounts as well
column6: allowing guests to perform these same actions. Other default configurations allow for Self-Service creation of accounts from accepted mail domains."^"True"^"False"^"Critical"^" "^"The excessive user permissions can be mitigated by running the listed PowerShell commands as a Global Admin. User access to the Azure AD blade can be restricted by navigating to the Azure Active Directory blade → User Settings and toggling the 'Restrict access to Azure AD administration portal' to Yes. Guest invites may be restricted by navigating to the Azure Active Directory blade → External Identities → External Collaboration Settings
column7: or by going to the Azure Active Directory blade → User Settings → Manage external collaboration settings and toggling 'Members can invite' and 'Guests can invite' to No."^"Directory Self Service Signup : https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/enterprise-users/directory-self-service-signup;Limit User Access : https://o365blog.com/post/limit-user-access/;Azure AD - Attack of the Default Config : https://www.pentestpartners.com/security-blog/azure-ad-attack-of-the-default-config/;Azure AD Default Configuration Blunders : https://medium.com/soteria-security/azure-ad-default-configuration-blunders-c7abddeae56"^" "^" "^" "^" "^" "

column4: as there is a chance IMAP is used within the organization for legacy applications or service accounts."^"Configure mailbox access (POP3 and IMAP) : https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/exchange/clients/pop3-and-imap4/configure-mailbox-access;Set-CASMailbox Commandlet Reference : https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/exchange/set-casmailbox?view=exchange-ps;Federal Bureau of Investigation Business Email Compromise Mitigation Recommendations : https://www.ic3.gov/Media/Y2020/PSA200406"^" "^" "^" "^" "^" "

column1: 11^"Malware Filter Policies Don't Alert for Internal Users Sending Malware"^"Malware filter named Default; Admin Address: $null
column2: NotificationsEnabled: False"^"Malware being sent by an internal user's email account is often an indication that a security event has occurred. For this reason
column3: it is strongly recommended that each organization have malware filter policies that alert administrators when malware is being sent by an internal user's account. It is possible to configure malware filter policies in O365 that generate these alerts. The malware filter policies listed herein do not alert an administrator when an internal user sends malware."^"False"^"True with a configured mailbox or distribution list address"^"Critical"^" "^"It is possible to configure malware filter policies from within the Exchange Online Protection portal or through the Set-MalwareFilterPolicy command as listed below. Make sure the policy is set to email a service account or administrator that is active and monitored and consider establishing a retention period for which to store malware alerts
column4: as they may be evidence in future security incidents."^"Configuring Exchange Online Protection : https://practical365.com/first-steps-configuring-exchange-online-protection/;Set-MalwareFilterPolicy Commandlet Reference (see Example 1 especially) : https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/exchange/set-malwarefilterpolicy?view=exchange-ps"^" "^" "^" "^" "^" "

column1: 12^"No Conditional Access Policies Block Risky Sign-in"^"No policies exist to control user sign-in with a risk level of medium or higher"^"No Conditional Access Policies were found that block users with a sign-in risk of medium of higher. This configuration attempts to determine if the sign-in is being attempted by anyone other than the legitimate user. When not configured
column2: sign-in events that would indicate compromise (impossible travel
column3: etc.) are permitted."^"None"^"Configured Conditional Access Policy"^"Critical"^" "^"Create or modify Conditional Access Policies to include defined sign-in risk values. An example configuration: Navigate to Azure Active Directory/Microsoft Entra → Navigate to Conditional Access (Security → Conditional Access in Azure Active Directory/Protect & Secure → Conditional Access in Microsoft Entra) → Select '+ New Policy' → Select Users → 'All Users' → Select 'Cloud apps or actions' → Select 'All cloud apps' → Select 'Conditions' → Select 'Sign-in risk' → Select 'Yes' to configure → Select desired risk configurations → Select 'Grant Controls' → Select 'Block access' → Change the 'Enable policy' value to 'On' → Select 'Create'."^"What is Conditional Access in Azure Active Directory? : https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/conditional-access/overview;Common Conditional Access Policies : https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/conditional-access/concept-conditional-access-policy-common;Raising the Baseline Security for all Organizations in the World : https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/t5/azure-active-directory-identity/raising-the-baseline-security-for-all-organizations-in-the-world/ba-p/3299048"^" "^" "^" "^" "^" "

14^"No Transport Rules to Block Executable Attachments"^"M365x36063842"^"No Exchange Online Transport Rules are in place to block email containing executable attachments. Executable attachments can be used to deliver malicious payloads and exfiltrate company data."^"None"^"Configured Transport Rule"^"Critical"^" "^"Navigate to the Mail Flow → Rules screen in the Exchange Admin Center. Add a rule that applies when the message contains executable attachments and takes the action of blocking the message. An article in the References section also describes this process."^"Manage Mail Flow Rules in Exchange Online : https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/exchange/security-and-compliance/mail-flow-rules/manage-mail-flow-rules"^" "^" "^" "^" "^" "

15^"No Transport Rules to Block Large Attachments"^"M365x36063842"^"No Exchange Online Transport Rules are in place to block emails with overly large attachments. Emails with overly large attachments may present a security risk for several reasons. Emails the domains receive may have overly large attachments that contain malware
column2: and adversaries sometimes use overly large files in an attempt to bypass anti-malware scanners or otherwise avoid suspicion. An adversary with access to an organizational email account may also use a large attachment to exfiltrate sensitive data from the organization; for example
column3: emailing an encrypted archive file to other adversarial infrastructure using a compromised O365 account. It is often recommended to create a rule that detects and blocks attachments over a certain size for these reasons."^"None"^"Configured Transport Rule"^"Critical"^" "^"Go to the Exchange Mail Flow rules screen and create a new rule which blocks attachments over a designated size."^"Common Attachment Blocking Scenarios : https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/exchange/security-and-compliance/mail-flow-rules/common-attachment-blocking-scenarios"^" "^" "^" "^" "^" "

column1: 16^"Safe Links Click-Through is Allowed"^"Built-In Protection Policy: True"^"Advanced Threat Protection Safe Links (ATP Safe Links) is an Office 365 feature that enables the detection of suspicious links used in attacks delivered via Exchange Email and Teams
column2: such as phishing attacks. ATP Safe Links is configured to allow users to click through a link flagged as unsafe if they choose. It is recommended to disable this ability
column3: as users will often click through to potentially unsafe links if they are given the choice
column4: partially negating the benefit of Safe Links."^"True"^"False"^"Critical"^" "^"Use the Set-SafeLinksPolicy function in the Exchange Online PowerShell module as follows: Set-SafeLinksPolicy -AllowClickThrough $false."^"Set-SafeLinksPolicy Reference : https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/exchange/set-safelinkspolicy?view=exchange-ps"^" "^" "^" "^" "^" "

column1: 17^"Third-Party Applications Allowed"^"True"^"Third-party integrated applications are allowed to run in the organization's Office 365 environment if a user authorizes them to do so. This configuration is considered insecure because a user may grant permissions to a malicious application without fully understanding the security implications. A user who installs a malicious third-party application is in effect compromised. Additionally
column2: there are documented cases of a malicious actor gaining access to sensitive information by enticing a user to allow a third-party integrated application to run within their O365 Tenant."^"True"^"False"^"Critical"^" "^"In the Office 365 administration center
column3: navigate to the Org Settings page
column4: then select Services → User Consent to Apps and turn user consent off."^"Managing user consent to apps in Microsoft 365 : https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-365/admin/misc/user-consent?view=o365-worldwide;Detect and Remediate Illicit Content Grants : https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-365/security/office-365-security/Inspect-and-remediate-illicit-consent-grants?view=o365-worldwide"^" "^" "^" "^" "^" "

column1: 23^"No Spam Filters to Flag Emails containing IP Addresses as Spam"^"Default: Off"^"The organization does not have Exchange Spam/Content Filter policies to flag emails containing IP addresses as spam. Cyber adversaries often send emails that contain the IP addresses of malicious websites or other attack infrastructure. This may occur during phishing or lateral phishing. For this reason it is advisable to create Exchange Transport Rules to detect IP addresses in email as spam."^"Off"^"On"^"High"^" "^"Navigate to the Exchange Admin Center's 'Protection' menu
column2: then click the 'spam filter' section. Create a spam policy or modify the default spam policy such that it is activated when numeric IP addresses are in URLs. The Set-HostedContentFilterPolicy commandlet may also be used to configure these settings. These settings may soon be replaced by the Advanced Spam Filter (ASF) settings in Exchange Online Protection."^"Configuring Exchange Online Protection
column3: First Steps : https://practical365.com/first-steps-configuring-exchange-online-protection/;Advanced Spam Filter (ASF) Settings in Exchange Online Protection : https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-365/security/office-365-security/advanced-spam-filtering-asf-options?view=o365-worldwide;Set-HostedContentFilterPolicy Commandlet Reference : https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/exchange/set-hostedcontentfilterpolicy?view=exchange-ps"^" "^" "^" "^" "^" "

column1: 24^"No Transport Rules to Block Domain Spoofing"^"M365x36063842"^"No Exchange Online Transport Rules are in place to block emails that are spoofing Tenant owned domains. It is recommended to speak with stakeholders and determine if this type of rule is beneficial and if any exceptions are needed. Microsoft configures some Anti-Spoofing settings by default in the Anti-Phishing policies on tenants

column2: this connection may enable bidirectional sharing of the user's profile data
